questions.question: Define Pushdown Automaton and list its seven components.
keyPoints: PDA is a computational model extending finite automaton with unbounded stack memory
Components: Q (finite set of states), Σ (input alphabet), Γ (stack alphabet), δ (transition function), q₀ (start state), Z₀ (initial stack symbol), F (accepting states)

questions.question: Explain the two methods of acceptance in a Pushdown Automaton.
keyPoints: Acceptance by Final State: PDA accepts if after consuming entire input, it reaches a state in F
Acceptance by Empty Stack: PDA accepts if after consuming entire input, the stack becomes empty (contains only Z₀ which is popped)

questions.question: What is an Instantaneous Description (ID) in a PDA? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: ID is a triple (q, w, α) representing complete PDA configuration
q = current state, w = remaining input string, α = current stack contents (top on right)
Example: (q₀, ab, XY) means at state q₀, input 'ab' remaining, stack has X on bottom, Y on top

questions.question: Differentiate between Deterministic and Non-deterministic PDA.
keyPoints: DPDA: For any configuration, at most one possible move (δ has at most one element)
NPDA: Multiple possible moves from same configuration allowed (δ returns a set)
DPDA ⊂ NPDA in terms of language recognition power
DPDA languages are deterministic CFLs, NPDA recognizes all CFLs

questions.question: What are ε-moves in a PDA? When and why are they used?
keyPoints: ε-moves are transitions that consume no input symbol
Allow PDA to change state and modify stack without reading input
Used for: moving between states without consuming input, performing stack operations when convenient, implementing non-deterministic choices
Syntax: δ(q, ε, X) contains moves

questions.question: Explain the transition function δ of a PDA with an example.
keyPoints: δ: Q × (Σ ∪ {ε}) × Γ → P(Q × Γ*) defines possible moves
Given current state, input symbol (or ε), and top stack symbol, returns new state and replacement string
Example: δ(q₀, a, Z₀) = {(q₁, XZ₀)} means: at q₀, read 'a', replace Z₀ with XZ₀ (push X)

questions.question: Why is a PDA more powerful than a Finite Automaton? Give a language example.
keyPoints: FA has finite memory (only states), PDA has unbounded stack memory
PDA can count arbitrarily large amounts using stack (LIFO)
Example: {aⁿbⁿ | n≥1} - FA cannot count, but PDA can push n 'a's then pop matching 'b's
This language is context-free but not regular

questions.question: Provide a formal definition of Pushdown Automaton. Explain each component with its purpose.
keyPoints: Formal definition: M = (Q, Σ, Γ, δ, q₀, Z₀, F)
Q: Finite non-empty set of control states
Σ: Input alphabet (finite set of input symbols)
Γ: Stack alphabet (finite set of symbols that can be on stack)
δ: Transition function defining moves
q₀: Start state (initial control state)
Z₀: Initial stack symbol (bottom marker, always in Γ)
F: Set of accepting/final states (F ⊆ Q)
Explanation of each component's role in computation

questions.question: Design a PDA (give formal specification) that accepts the language L = {0ⁿ1ⁿ | n ≥ 1} by empty stack. Explain its working.
keyPoints: Define PDA M = (Q, Σ, Γ, δ, q₀, Z₀, F)
δ(q₀, 0, Z₀) = {(q₀, 0Z₀)} - push 0 for each 0 in input
δ(q₀, 0, 0) = {(q₀, 00)} - keep pushing 0s
δ(q₀, 1, 0) = {(q₁, ε)} - start popping when 1s appear
δ(q₁, 1, 0) = {(q₁, ε)} - keep popping for each 1
δ(q₁, ε, Z₀) = {(q₁, ε)} - accept when stack empty
Working: Push all 0s, then pop one 0 for each 1, accept if both exhausted simultaneously

questions.question: Explain the relationship between Pushdown Automata and Context-Free Languages. What are the limitations of DPDA?
keyPoints: Theorem: Language L is context-free iff some NPDA recognizes L
NPDA = equivalent to CFL in recognition power
Every regular language is also context-free (regular ⊂ CFL)
DPDA limitation: Recognizes only deterministic CFLs (a proper subset)
Not all CFLs are deterministic - e.g., WWᵀ (palindromes) cannot be recognized by DPDA
DPDA has less expressive power than NPDA in terms of languages accepted

questions.question: What is meant by a configuration of a PDA? Explain the notation for PDA moves. How do we represent computation as a sequence of configurations?
keyPoints: Configuration = Instantaneous Description = (q, w, α)
q = current state, w = remaining input, α = stack contents (leftmost = bottom)
Move notation: (p, a, X) ⊢ (q, ε, γ) means from state p, read a, pop X, go to q, push γ
⊢* denotes zero or more moves (reflexive transitive closure)
Computation sequence: (q₀, w, Z₀) ⊢* (q, ε, ε) for empty stack acceptance
Computation sequence: (q₀, w, Z₀) ⊢* (q_f, ε, α) for final state acceptance where q_f ∈ F
